column7:
Notes
(explanation of ordinances with dates on when proposed/created/enforced)

Santa Monica had an existing, unenforceable camping ordinance. In September 2024, they amended it to reinstate and enforce it. 4.08.095 Prohibition against camping in public places. Ordinance extends even to sleeping bags, pillows, and blankets.
Existing codes 99.01-99.04 banned camping, which was unenforceable until Grants Pass decision. Enforcement has likely begun again, as shown in a July 2024 internal staff memo recommending it.

10/31/24 Press release said that anti-camping ordinances will be enforced post Grants Pass. Language: "Enforcing Public Camping & Loitering Regulations: In line with the recent Supreme Court Grants Pass decision, local laws prohibit camping or sleeping in vehicles on public streets and overnight camping on the beach. Hermosa Beach also enforces laws that prevent loitering in public spaces that obstruct pedestrian movement or cause quality of life impacts."  - Hermosa is currently considering a new ordinance - return!
City of Arcadia website - Arcadia’s anti-camping laws paused enforcement after *Martin v. Boise*, and have resumed post-Grants Pass
Existing ordinance 9.28.010-9.28.040 prohibited camping. Glendale city council voted to resume enforcement.

Long Beach PD now has discretion to enforce existing anti-camping ordinance. Internal memo primarily discusses citations, but also arrests, especially following repeated complaints or "related criminal conduct." Long Beach has been conducting widespread sweeps.

Per Malibu city website - said that Grants Pass won't change much, but did specify 72-hour notices to vacate encampments on public property, when they violate 8.36.050. Will cite unhoused people with "cite and release" system.
2024 election - Irwindale passed a measure for a slight sales tax increase to fund, among many other things, "cleaning up homeless encampments."
Arcadia had an existing camping ordinance that was unenforceable under Martin. Per their website, they will resume enforcement.

San Dimas actually amended their municipal code (not just changing enforcement for an additional code, post Grants Pass). That's code 1310. It is standard language, authorizing citations for storage of belongings on public property and erecting campsites/using camp equpment, with general language about promoting the health and safety of the community.

Probably enforcing their standard 2016 camping ordinance. Although the most recent news (September 2024) only said that the mayor and City Council were "discussing" actions in light of Grants Pass, and that they were not criminalizing homelessness, they did receive a $2.7 million "Encampment Resolution" grant from the state in October.
Actually adopted a new ordinance - "Prohibit Encampments and Storage of Personal Property in Public Places" - prosecution as a misdemeanor or public nuisance. 72 hour notice of sweeps.
Signal Hill updated its camping ordinance in August 2024 to enforce anti-camping post-Grants Pass. Extends to camping, "camp facilities," and "camp paraphernalia.
Torrance updated its camping ordinance to prohibit camping in public places, fires in parks and public places, "maintaining shelters," and "loitering at bus stops."

Whittier had an existing camping ordinance and released a statement of support post-Grants Pass, though hasn't updated its municipal code. Is probably enforcing the existng ordinance, which extends to bedding. Ordinance no. 3105.
Impact: Whittier kept a shelter open in order to allow its camping ordinance to be enforced under Boise, but is now considering having fewer beds available because of Grants Pass.

As Long Beach implemented city-wide sweeps, a 55-year-old man named Fernando Gonzalex was displaced. The city did not find him a bed. https://www.latimes.com/california/story/2024-08-26/long-beach-homeless-encampments-cleared
Redondo Beach, in accordance with Grants Pass, may restrict camping near supportive housing.
Manhattan Beach's camping provision (4.140), enacted shortly before Boise, and includes pillows and sleeping materials, and personal property, is now being enforced.

The City of LA has been resisting Newsom's encampment clearance policies, however already had an anti-camping ordinance on the books, pre-Boise. Reporting on that ordinance says that it cost $3 million and permanently housed two people.
9/17/24 - El Segundo City Council amended the municipal code with new camping ordinances. Encampments on public property will be given 24 hour clearance notices. PATCHES team offers outreach.

Aug. 2024
City of Carlsbad accepted $5.3 mil from Encampment Resolution Funding Program to "address" State Route 78 encampments.

Aug. 2024
City of Carlsbad accepted $5.3 mil from Encampment Resolution Funding Program to "address" State Route 78 encampments.

Jan. 2025
40 Encampents cleared. 672 people "served," 133 helped with transition to housing (not specified if housing secured).

Aug. 2024
Previous ordinance required that officers offer shelter. City considering updating ordinance – likely to be related to giving out citations without available shelter.
Statement by Carlsbad City Councilmember Burkholder: “Our understanding . . . was essentially that we didn't have to offer shelter before we gave a citation to folks."

Definitions
"'Campsite' means any place where
(1) bedding, sleeping bag, or other material used for bedding purposes,
(2) or any stove or fire is placed, established, or maintained for the purpose of maintaining a temporary or permanent place to live, whether or not such place incorporates the use of any tent, lean-to, shack or any other structure."
 Chula Vista, Cal., Code § 9.90.020 (2024)

Prohibited Conduct
Prohibits Sitting, Lying, Sleeping, or Storage on a street, sidewalk, or public property "in a manner that impedes passage, as provided by the ADA."
Chula Vista, Cal., Code § 9.90.030 (2024).

Prohibited Conduct
Cannot camp
(1) w/in 1000 ft of a school,
(2) in or w/in 1000 ft of a city park,
(3) wildlife conservation areas,
(4) "Major Transit Stops," lists 3 trolley stops and 225 bus stops,
(5) w/in 1000 ft of a shelter/safe camping/safe parking site.
Chula Vista, Cal., Code § 9.90.040 (2024)

Enforcement
(1) police may remove personal property; (2) camping is a public nuisance; (3) violation may be charged as misdemeanor or infraction.

Notice Requirements
(1) At least 3 hrs written notice before violating 9.90.030; At least 24 hrs written notice before removal of property
(2) If City Manager/Police Department finds it "reasonably necessary to remove [a] immediate threat," or if SDC directs city to remove property for public health and safety, may remove property w/out any notice.
Chula Vista, Cal., Code § 9.90.050 (2024).

Prohibited Conduct
(1) No camping/sleeping in public parks/beaches 11:00pm-5:00am.
(2) No campling/sleeping on any public street
(3) No loitering on public streets 11:00pm-5:00am.
Coronado, Cal., Code § 40.48.010 (2015).

Prohibited Conduct
(1) No tents/canopies/barricades in public parks/beaches 11pm-5am
(2) Can't leave tents/canopies/barricades unattended 5am-11pm
Coronado, Cal., Code § 40.48.012 (2015).

Prohibited Conduct
Can't stand/sit on street/median/sidewalk in a manner that obstructs passage or to "hinder/molest/annoy" passerbys
Coronado, Cal., Code § 40.48.040.

Prohibited Conduct
(1) No camping overnight.
(2) Camping evidenced by bedrolls and tents
Del Mar, Cal., Code § 9.04.020.

Feb. 2025
After a fire at a homeless encampment in an unincorporated area of El Cajon, several arrests were made for charges "including drug-related offenses, outstanding misdemeanor warrants and identity theft" as the encampment was cleared

Definitions
(1) Camping in parks = living/spending inordinary amount of time in a park with "camp paraphenelia"
(2) Camp paraphenelia "includ[es], but [is] not limited to, clothing, sleeping bags, bedrolls, blankets, sheets, luggage, backpacks, kitchen utensils, cookware, and similar material"
El Cajon, Cal. Code § 2.56.010.

Prohibited Conduct
Prohibits sleeping or camping on city property
El Cajon, Cal. Code §§ 9.36.020, 9.36.025.

Prohibited Conduct
(1) Cannot occupy public street/sidewalk/alley for any purpose besides normal pedestrian traffic.
(2) May not loiter in a manner that "maliciously obstruct[s] or interfere[s] with free passage."
El Cajon, Cal. Code § 12.08.010-20.

Introduced Feb. 2025
Proposes collaboration between CA DOT and local gov'ts to clear existing homeless encampments on DOT property and prevent new encampments from forming.
S.B. 569

Prohibited Conduct
(1) No camping on public property
Encinitas, Cal. Code § 9.01.020 (2004).

Definitions
"'Camping' is evidenced by, among other things, the establishment of sleeping accommodations; the use of a bedroll or sleeping bag; the erection or maintenance of a shelter"
Encinitas, Cal. Code § 9.01.020 (2004).

Dec. 2024
Encampment of 52 cleared out. City of Escondido has only 15 homeless shelter beds

Definitions
Camping = "remaining for prolonged or repetitious periods of time not associated with ordinary recreational use of a park with one's personal property (including, but not limited to, clothing, sleeping bags, bedrolls, blankets, sheets, tents, tarpaulins, luggage, backpacks, kitchen utensils, cookware, and similar material), sleeping or making preparations to sleep, regularly cooking or consuming meals, or living in a parked vehicle"
Escondido, Cal. Code § 17-7

Prohibited Conduct
No camping
(1) where there's an "unreasonable risk of harm" to people/public health/public safety or a disruption of vital gov't services
(2) w/in 500 ft of a school zone
(3) w/in 500 ft of a shelter if there's visible signage
(4) In any "open space, waterway, or bank of a waterway."
(5) in transit hubs(7) any park or open space where city manager considered camping to pose a substantial public health and safety
Escondido, Cal. Code § 17-8.4 (2024).

Enforcement
(1) Violation is a misdemeanor offense
(2) Subject to removal after 24 hr. written notice/expedited abatement
Escondido, Cal. Code § 17-8.5 (2024).

Prohibited Conduct
(1) Prohibits camping, lodging, or sleeping on or w/in any public property.
(2) Prohibits storage of personal items on public proeprty
Imperial Beach, Cal. Code § 12.5.

Jan. 2025
La Mesa Councilmember calls for policy of removing a homeless person from the city if help is turned down 15 times. No bill.

Definitions
(1) Camping is not limited to tents and can be anywhere where bedding is placed
(2) No person shall camp in or upon any city sidewalk, street, alley, lane, park, public right-of-way or any other place to which the general public has access, or under any bridgeway or viaduct
La Mesa, Cal. Code §§ 10.52.020, 10.52.030

Aug. 2024
City has failed to deliver on commitment to create homeless shelter over period of 3 years

Prohibited Conduct
Prohibits camping or storage of property in any public place or street
Lemon Grove, Cal. Code §§ 9.36.090.

Enforcement
Violation = misdemeanor + fine < $500 or prison < 6 mo.
Lemon Grove, Cal. Code § 9.36.100.

Oct. 2024
National City begins enforcing new anti-camping and -sleeping ordinance

Definitions
Has adopted San Diego City's code.
National City, Cal. Code § 10.55.020 (2024).

Prohibited Conduct
Has adopted San Diego City's code.
National City, Cal. Code § 10.55.040 (2024).

Enforcement
Has adopted San Diego City's code.
National City, Cal. Code §§ 10.55.050, 10.55.060 (2024).

Oct. 2024
Oceanside PD begins enforcement of new anti-encampment ordinances w/out bed availability

Definitions
(1) Camping = "to pitch or occupy camp facilities, or to use camp paraphernalia, for living purposes in an outdoor area." NOT sleeping alone
(2) Camp facilities = tents/huts/shacks/other temp.shelters
(3) Camp paraphernalia = but is not limited to, tents or tent-like structures, cots, beds, hammocks or personal cooking facilities and similar equipment. NOT blankets/sleeping bags/bedrolls
(4) Obstruct ped./veh. traffic.= "take evasive action to avoid physical contact"
(5) Open space = unimproved and designated for open space use
Oceanside, Cal. Code § 20.21.1(b) (2024).

Prohibited Conduct
(1) Cannot camp/occupy/use camp paraphenelia in a public space
(2) Cannot sit "on . . . any object" in a manner that obstructs ped./veh. traffic
(3) Cannot sleep in open space
(4) Cannot cook food in public space
Oceanside, Cal. Code § 20.21.1(c) (2024).

Prohibited Conduct
Cannot store personal property (including bed rolls) on public property unattended
Oceanside, Cal. Code § 202.21.2(b) (2024)

Enforcement
(1) Property may be removed after 24 hr written notice
(2) Property may be reclaimed w/in 90 days by identifying property and approx. location it was left
Oceanside, Cal. Code § 20.21.2(d) (2024).

Definitions
Camping is
(1) Residing in or using any public area or private property for living accommodation or habitation purposes
(2) constructing/maintaining/using camping facilities or paraphenelia even if not in active use (bedding included)
Poway, Cal. Code § 8.26.020 (2023)

Prohibited Activities
Cannot camp on public property or private property w/ permission
Poway, Cal. Code § 8.26.030 (2023).

Enforcement
(1) 24 hrs. written notice before removal
(2) Property may be reclaimed w/in 90 days (unclear on what instructions will provided to owners)
Poway, Cal. Code § 8.26.070 (2023).

Feb. 2025
City begins plan to clear homeless encampments in brush areas b/c fire risk. City says that assistance will be offered when notice is given but does not state that alternative housing will be provided.

Definitions
(1) "Camp" or "Camping" means to place, pitch, erect, or occupy an encampment, or to use camp paraphernalia or both, for the purpose of, or in a way that facilitates outdoor sheltering for living, accommodation or habitation purposes or for remaining outdoors overnight.
(2) "Camp Paraphernalia" includes, but is not limited to, tarps, cots, beds, sleeping bags, hammocks, bedrolls, camp stoves, cooking equipment, buckets, and similar equipment or items that are accessory to encampments, mattresses, couches, dressers, or other
San Diego, Cal. Code § 63.0402 (2024).

Prohibited Conduct
Public nuisance to camp, use camp paraphenelia or accumulate waste
(1) on public property
(2) w/in 2 blocks of a school
(3) in waterways
(4) w/in transit hubs
San Diego, Cal. Code § 63.0404 (2024).

Enforcement
(1) Encampments may be removed
(2) Violation is a misdemeanor
San Diego, Cal. Code §§ 63.0405-06 (2024).

Has adopted San Diego City's code
San Marcos, Cal. Code § 10.33 (2024)

Defintions
Camping includes use of bedding
Santee, Cal. Code § 7.20.020 (2019).

Prohibited Conduct
(1) sitting or lying down on the sidewalk or any object placed on the sidewalk in most city zones
(2) loitering where no loitering signs have been posted and verban notice has been given
(3) loitering around public transportation facilities after 48 hr. verbal notice
(4) camping w/in 1000 ft of a waterbody
(5) camping in a park
(6) camping on sidewalks or streets in a manner that obstructs traffic
Santee, Cal. Code §§ 7.20.030-60

2025
(1) 72-hr notice given before camp is abated
(2) Proeprty may be recovered w/in 90 days

Prohibited Conduct
(1) Unlawful to pitch tent/create temp. structure in public space
(2) May not sleep in public space within any camp/shelter
Solana Beach, Cal. Code §§ 6.24.010-20 (

March 2025- San Diego Board of Supervisors passed Unsafe Camping Ordinance for 2025 - The board, after approving the motion in a 4-0 vote Tuesday, will review and vote on a draft ordinance in two months. If that is approved, it will require a second vote to become law under county policy.

Aug. 2024
City to start enforcing 1968 anti-camping ban
Nov. 2024 Unanimous vote to remove the exception in their anti-camping law (no beds needed in shelters to arrest for homelessness)
In 2022 Annaheim was responsible for 20% of the 496 homeless deaths in Orange County
Aug. 2024 Greater prohibitions on sleeping in public places, setting up camps, and leaving property unattended
March 2025 Further tightening on anti-camping laws, criminalizing of homeless people
"We’ve got a lot of carrots, but I think it’s time to give our police department also the stick" quote from City counselman
Approved Nov. 2024, "prohibits camping, sleeping, storing personal property on public places, or interfering with public access to public places"
Passed Aug. 2024 further restricting camping
3/11/2024 (pre-GP) there was a hearing to adjust their park rules, no overnight camping in parks
Seems like the anti-homelessness laws have been strict and unchanged since 2022
7,300 Homeless people counted in Orange County in January 2024
Nov 2024 Irvine has no homeless shelter, but ability clear out homeless shelters with only 24 hours notice,
Didn't pass a ban on feeding homeless people in February 2025

Unlawful camping ordinance. Specifies that sleeping on its own does not constitute camping, but includes sleeping bags and bedrolls as "camp paraphernalia" such that if sleeping bags/bedrolls are used "in such a way as will facilitate sleeping," it is unlawful camping. Adopted 4/23/2024 (pre-Grants Pass)

Sitting, lying, sleeping in public places is unlawful if obstructing traffic, between 6am and 10pm. Includes sleeping in vehicle parked in public. From 10pm to 6am, sleeping prohibited in public UNLESS no sleeping/shelter spaces practically available. Adopted 4/23/2024 (pre-Grants Pass)
Unlawful camping/unlawful storage of personal property ordinance. Encapsulates public/private property. Includes bedrolls as "camp paraphernalia." Adopted 9/3/2024 (post-Grants Pass)
Sitting, lying, sleeping, or storing property in public right of way prohibited. Adopted 10/6/2021.
Camping prohibited on public/private property, including sleeping in a vehicle. Adopted 7/10/2013.

Two proposed ordinances related to homelessness in November 2024 city council meeting agenda. Agenda explicitly references Grants Pass decision as motivation to prepare ordinances "specifically related to homelessness." One prohibits unlawful camping/using camp paraphernalia on public property, obstructing right of ways, sitting and lying on public benches, & public urination/defecation; the other declares shopping cart use for personal belongings a public nuisance
Prohibits sleeping vehicles parked in public places. Adopted in 1982.
Prohibits camping/using camp paraphernalia and storing personal property in public. "Camp paraphernalia" includes sleeping bags. Adopted 2/10/2016.
Camping and storage of personal property prohibited on public property. (`78 Code, § 9.26.020.) (Ord. 3160 § 4, 2013; Ord. 2833 § 3 (part), 2006; Ord. 2619 § 1, 2002: Ord. 2256 § 1, 1995.)

Unlawful to camp or use camp paraphernalia (including sleeping bags) on any street, public parking lot, or private property, and to store personal property in any park, street, public property, or private property without owner/tenant consent. Adopted 12/3/2019.
Prohibits camping/using camp paraphernalia and storing personal property in public. "Camp paraphernalia" includes sleeping bags and bed rolls. Adopted 9/5/2024 (post-Grants Pass)

Under "disorderly conduct," prohibits obstructing public property by sitting/lying/storing personal property (adopted 1/11/2022), eating/resting as well as sleeping in a vehicle (adopted 7/8/2003), and camping on private property (adopted 1/11/2022)
Prohibits camping/using camp paraphernalia and storing personal property in public. "Camp paraphernalia" includes sleeping bags and bed rolls. Adopted 2012.

Prohibits camping and storing personal property in public spaces. Camping explicitly includes sleeping "for living accommodation purposes" and occurs whether or not a full night is spent. Amended 8/21/2024 (post-Grants Pass). Staff report discussing amendment references Grants Pass decision as motivation for amendment.

Prohibits camping/using camp paraphernalia and storing personal property in public. "Camp paraphernalia" includes sleeping bags and bed rolls. Adopted 9/5/2024 (post-Grants Pass). Exception for "emergency public health and safety activities."

Camping/remaining overnight prohibited in public parks and recreation areas. One of many provisions under "prohibited conduct" in parks/rec section. No specific language appearing to target homeless population. (Ord. 568 § 8, 2018; Ord. 510 § 1, 2013; Ord. 360 § 1, 2001; Ord. 10 § 1, 1982)

No comprehensive unlawful camping ordinance, but three separate ordinances that are relevant. One prohibits camping on private property without owner's consent (adopted 2015), one prohibits camping on public parks and beaches (adopted 2016), and one declares shopping carts a public nuisance (adopted 2016).

Prohibits camping/using camp paraphernalia and storing personal property in public. "Camp paraphernalia" includes sleeping bags and bed rolls. Sets out procedure to refer homeless people sitting/lying/sleeping on public property when no shelter is available to "Outreach & Engagement Personnel" before arresting or citing them. BUT states that "These procedures under § 11.21.030(B)(2) are only mandatory during the timeframe that the case of Martin v. City of Boise 902 F.3d 1031 (9th Cir. 2018) is applicable law within the jurisdiction of the Ninth Circuit." Adopted 10/20/2021.

Prohibits camping/using camp paraphernalia and storing personal property on public streets, sidewalks, right-of-ways, parks, and any other improved or unimproved public land. "Camp paraphernalia" includes sleeping bags and bed rolls. Adopted 2004.

"Camp space and squatter camps" ordinance prohibitng camping on public/private property unless a number of sanitation requirements are met. Camping defined differently than in many other codes: "'Camp space' is any place where one or more tents are erected or maintained for hire for living or sleeping quarters for one or more families, or where one or more tents or camp cars or trailers or where one or more families are camping with the expressed consent of the owner or person legally in charge of the land upon which such camp space is located." Adopted 1964.
Prohibits camping/using camp paraphernalia and storing personal property in public. "Camp paraphernalia" includes sleeping bags and bed rolls. Adopted 2009.

Explicitly bans encampments & lays out specific procedures for clearing them. Explicitly bans encampments regardless of shelter bed availability in a number of situations, including "unreasonable risk to harm or public safety," when there is a "Disruption to any business, store, religious institution, educational facility, or government services," and "in any open space, waterway, or banks of a waterway." Probably the most comprehensive and strict anti-homeless law I've encountered.

Two provisions under general sections that may or may not target homeless populations. Under use of public property: Unlawful to "camp, lodge or tarry overnight" on any public property. Terms not defined. Adopted 1970. Under use of park and recreation facilities: "No person shall tent, camp, lodge, or otherwise be present in any park or recreation facility after 11:00 p.m. or before 6:00 a.m." Adopted 1992.

Prohibits camping/using camp paraphernalia and storing personal property in public. "Camp paraphernalia" includes sleeping bags. Enforcement: public nuisance (not infraction/misdemeanor). Adopted 2019.

One ordinance prohibits camping/using camp paraphernalia in public spaces. "Camp paraphernalia" includes sleeping bags. Enforcement: public nuisance (not infraction/misdemeanor). Adopted 2022. Another prohibits sitting/lying on sidewalks, lying on public benches, and distributing food to large groups of people w/o permit. Adopted 2021.
Prohibits camping/using camp paraphernalia (including bed rolls and sleeping bags) in public spaces. Also prohibits lying on public benches.
Prohibits camping without a permit, although doesn't seem to target the homeless population. Also prohibits storing personal property in public spaces. Adopted 2018.
Prohibits sitting or lying on public ways
Prohibits sitting or lying on public ways / camping and sleeping outdoors or in a vehicle, or park a recreational vehicle overnight in a public location. Adopted 2005
Prohibits sitting or lying on public ways, adopted 1987 / prohibited camping in public park, Adpoted 2008-2010
Prohibited caamping and loitering, storage personal property, adopted in 2019. Penalty
Prohibits camping in city owned space / Enforcement
[Chino]
Prohibits camping or store personal property on public property / Enforcement. Adopted in 2011

Prohibits lingering, loitering, sitting, or standing on commercial or private property(Ch 12.26.)
Prohibits standing or sitting in roadway(Sec 17-228)
Prohibits loitering. adopted 1988
no ordinances re camping/loitering
no ordinances re camping/loitering
no ordinances re camping/loitering
no ordinances re camping/loitering
no ordinances re camping/loitering

Prohibited camping in park, street, public parking area. Adopted 1995 and amended 2010
Prohibited storing personal property. Adopted 1995
Prohibited loitering in designated public places or in front of buildings. Adopted in 2008
Prohibited camping and storage of personal property in public places. Enforcement. Adopted in 2015
no ordinances re camping/loitering
Prohibited camping and storing personal property in public places. Adopted 1995
no ordinances re camping/loitering
Prohibits camping or store personal property on public property / Enforcement. Adopted in 2022
Prohibits camping or store personal property on public property / Penalties. Adopted in 2023
Prohibits camping or store personal property on public property / Enforcement. Adopted in 2023
camping in vehicle prohibited. Adopted in 2014

"Good Neighbor Program": The ordinance would prohibit camping within Water Resources Protection Zones, which include all lands where Valley Water owns property or has easements. Violations could result in fines of up to $500 and jail time of up to 30 days.

The ordinance would ban:
1. Unauthorized establishment of encampments
2. Any unauthorized presence in established zones after sunset
3. Depositing trash, debris, or hazardous pollutants related to encampments
4. Keeping animals in established zones
5. Activities that disturb neighboring properties or create hazards for Valley Water employees or the public

The city of San José has adopted new restrictions on encampments near schools:

1. The city council unanimously voted to ban all homeless encampments within 150 feet of K-12 schools citywide by establishing "School Clearance Zones".
2. This rule formalizes and slightly expands on a similar policy the city already had in place
3. Violators would be given a $0 administrative citation, with no criminal enforcement

San José has also implemented tougher rules on RV parking:

1. The city can now tow large vehicles in areas where parked cars create safety hazards for drivers, pedestrians, and cyclists.
2. This policy is particularly focused on areas near schools

Section 8-4.4 of the municipal code addresses camping specifically:

1. It states that “it is unlawful for any person to engage in camping in any City-owned property”
2. Where camping means “to set up, pitch, occupy, or leave camp facilities or use camp paraphernalia” (§8-4.2)
3. HOWEVER, it “will not be enforced against indigent homeless persons sitting, lying, or sleeping on City-owned public property” with some exceptions
4. This section of the municipal code appears to have been last amended on June 7, 2021

On Sept. 10, 2024, City Council approved legislation that broadened the city’s authority to sweep camps even when shelter isn’t available.

1. The resolution said that the city wouldn’t sweep encampments unless it was able to offer shelter to displaced people, but it also outlined several exceptions including encampments that posed a health or safety hazard.
2. Also on Sept. 10, 2024, nine business and property owners sued the city over a longstanding homeless encampment, citing public and private nuisances.
3. On Jan. 7, 2025, the city posted notice that officials had found such nuisance conditions at a longstanding encampment and directed everyone living there leave by Feb. 10 or the city would remove all property remaining on the property.
4. On Feb. 13, the Berkeley Homeless Union sued the city to enjoin Berkeley to sweep the encampment.
5. As of Feb. 14, a federal judge has temporarily blocked Berkeley from clearing the encampment.

Section 5.100.110 identifies pohibited acts/activities in parks. It states that "unless expressly authorized by the Director, no person while in any park shall: . . . E. Camp, lodge or remain overnight . . ."
Emeryville does not appear to have an ordinance specifically addressing to camping.

In February 2025, Fremont City Council gave final approval to an ordinance that bans camping throughout the entire city, while also making anyone "aiding, abetting or concelaing" a homeless encampment guilty of a misdemeanor. On March 4, they voted to remove the aiding and abetting clause after pushback from the community.

Article 17 of Hayward's municipal code addresses camping and storage of personal property:

1. It’s unlawful to camp, occupy camp facilities or use camp paraphernalia on any City-owned property. (Section 4-17.20)
2. It’s unlawful to store personal property, including camp facilities and camp paraphernalia on any City-owned property. (Section 4-17.25)
3. A violation is declared a public nuisance which may be enjoined by civil action or enforced with citations. (Section 4-17.30)
4. “Camp” means to pitch or occupy camp facilities; to use camp paraphernalia. (Section 4-17.10)
5. Appears to have been last updated in 2014.

Chapter 12.65 of the municipal code addresses camping. In livermore it's unlawful and a public nuisance to camp, establish/maintain/operate/occupy camping facilities, or use camp paraphernalia on any public property or any private proeprty where the public has right of access. It's simiarly unlawful to store camp paraphernalia on public property. aA violation is punishable by a fine up to $100 for a first offense, and as a misdemeanor punishable by a fine up to $300 or  up to 30 days in jail or both.
Section 7.04.030 talks about how unoccupied swellings are attractive for "unlawful camping and occuptation" but doesn't seem to clearly discuss unlawfulcamping elsewhere in the municipal code.

On Sept. 24, 2024, Oakland’s mayor gave an executive order to city departments to fully remove people living in homeless encampments, enforcing the Encampment Management Policy. The EMP was passed in 2020, prohibiting encampment within a certain number of feet of schools, parks, playgrounds, bodies of water and retail businesses. It also prohibits most of the cooking and heating equiment used by peope living in encampemnts.

Piedmont doesn't appear to have any ordinances specifically addressing camping or homelessness, aside from generally prohibiting obstructions of streets and sidewalks (Chapter 18, Article IV, Section 18.22)

(A) No person shall camp or lodge in a tent or on the ground in any park and recreation favility, including the parking lot area of any such facility.
(B) Except during daylight, or except for security purposes and with the written permission of the director, no person shall stay, remain or sleep in a motor home or other motor vehicle otherwise, in any park and recreation facility, including the parking lot area of such facility.

Ordinance No. 12-2024: an ordinance of the city council of the city of Elk Grove amending chapter 9.38 of the Elk Grove Municipal Code related to unlawful camping. Elk Grove City Council unanimously passed an amendment to a city ordinance Wednesday that shortens the window of notice city officials must give to those at homeless encampments, or unlawfully camping in the city, to 24 hours before temporarily seizing their property. The previous notice was a 72-hour window. The amendment, the city said, will allow them to respond quicker to camp clean-ups.

Elk Grove will also prohibit the following: “sleeping on public sidewalks, streets, or alleyways; sleeping in any pedestrian or vehicular entrance to public or private property abutting a public sidewalk; occupying a campsite upon any sidewalk, street, alley, lane, public right of way, park, bench, or any other public property and/or under any bridge; and camping on private property without the consent of the property owner.”

2.12.25 Ordinance - Mayor Lurie has authority to bypass traditional approvals to enact his agenda on homelessness. This ordinance temporarily eases procurement and approval rules to speed up the City’s response to homelessness, drug crises, mental health needs, and public safety hiring. Key provisions include:
- Procurement Waivers: Competitive bidding rules for related contracts, grants, and leases are suspended until January 2026.
- Mayoral Authority: The Mayor can approve certain contracts and leases if the Board of Supervisors does not act within 45 days.
- Lease Approvals: Board approval is not required for leases related to these initiatives until January 2026.
- Funding & Donations: The City can accept up to $10 million in private donations and grants for these efforts. The Mayor’s Office can also solicit donations for six months, overriding existing restrictions.
- Budget Flexibility: The Controller can reallocate surplus funds for these initiatives.
- Sunset Date: A prior waiver for homelessness-related contracts will expire in May 2029.

The Police Department determines if an encampment meets a certain threshold for posing a risk to public safety, public health, or environmental concerns. If a site meets the threshold, then the Police Department coordinates with Public Works to set the date for the encampment clean up, typically two weeks in advance. The Police Department then posts written “seven-day notices to vacate” throughout encampment locations advising of the cleanup date, the consequences of failure to vacate (including potential citation or arrest), and offering access to services. On the day of the clean-up, any remaining residents are given a final opportunity to vacate and collect their belongings before the clean-up begins and any enforcement action is taken. Personal property is stored for 90 days and the City allows owners to retrieve property during that time period.

The government is investing heavily to implement strategies in partnership with property owners, including the State, and service providers, including the County and a host of non-profit agencies.

In October, 2023, Solvang Mayor Mark Infanti signed a new ordinance to allow RVs to be parked in residential neighborhoods and along city streets with restrictions. It had been previously been prohibited. Under the new ordinance, temprorary parking is permitted for 24 hours a a time on city-owned public property, excluding areas within Solvang's Tourism Commercial District. Overnight parking is still prohibited downtown, near schools, or in areas that impede traffic. Once a vehicle has been parked in the same place for more than 24 hours, it will be asked to relocate.

The Clean Community Division of the City of Santa Barbara facillitates a program called "Clean SB" dedicated to keeping the City clean through programs focused on litter clean up, illegal dumping, abandonded shopping carts, and encampments. The response team for this program consists of representaties from City Net (a contractor) and represenatives from throughout the City. The team works to connect unsheltered individuals with services, clean up encampments, and take enforcement action. The team typically provides 72 hours advance notice of clean ups. The city reports 1,191 encampment cleanups since 2019. Notably absent is the amount of people housed from these encampments.

Chapter 9.98: Pedestrians Blocking Public Sidewalks. No person shall stand, or sit, or congregate in or upon any street, sidewalk or crosswalk in the City with the intent to hinder or obstruct the free passage of pedestrians thereon, or to annoy or molest such pedestrians, or to block the entrance to a building, and refuse to disperse after having been ordered to do so by the police when the police reasonably believe an immediate threat to public safety is present. Penalty is a misdemenaor, and punishment of a fine not exceeding $500, imprisonment for a term not exceeding six months, or both.

Chapet 9.97: Sitting or Lying on Sidewalks and Paseos Along Certain Downtown Portions of State Street. Deisgnates specific parts in downtown Santa Barbara where it is prohibited to sit or lie down on a sidewalk or public paseo, or upon a blanket, chair, stool, or any other object between 7:00am and 2:00am. Unclear from the chapter what the punishment is but it does state that no one can be prosecuted for a violation of this chapter unless the person engaged in the prohibited conudct after having been notified by a law enforcement officer or other City-designated volunteer or employee.

Chapter 9.50: Prohibition of Abusive Panhandling. This ordinance restricts abusive panhandling across the city of Snata Barbara. Abusive panhandling is defined as blocking or impeding someone's movement, following someone, threateneing them, touching the person, among other things. Furthermore, the ordinance restricts active panhandling (a verbal request) in many locations like bus stops, in a vehicle, in a City parking lot, within 25 feet of a restaurant, among other things. Any person who violates the ordinance shall be guilty of a misdemeanor, fined at most $1,000, or imprisoned for no more than 6 months, or both.

**Santa Barbara County, Not City** In January of this year, in the wake of the Grants Pass decision, the Santa Barbara County Supervisors adopted some changes, though not radical shifts, to their approach to dealing with encapments. Newly approved procedures stiipulated that encapments may only be cleared if alternative shelter is avaliable, with exceptions for publcic safety. These exceptions include encapments in waterways or areas where the use of flammables near dry brush heightens the risk of fire. Policy changes also allow for less notice before encampments are cleared and a shorter duration for storing uncollected belgomings.

Article XIIII of Salinas Municipal code (passed October 10, 2017) prohibits storing unattended personal property on city land, limits attended property to a volume fitting within a 90-gallon container, and bans storing items that obstruct pathways, entrances, or violate ADA requirements. Article XIV (passed October 10, 2017) prohibits camping in public areas.
The city can impound personal property with notice, or immediately remove items that pose health or safety threats or block essential areas. Notices must detail the removal process, property descriptions, and how property can be reclaimed within 90 days before disposal.
Additionally, the ordinance forbids attaching personal belongings to public or private property in a way that obstructs public movement, with immediate removal allowed.
The Salinas Homeless Union is one of the organizations based in Salinas advocates not only for visibly unhoused people but also for poor and vulnerable individuals living precariously according to the news of January 20, 2025.

Chapter 9.20 of Seaside Municipal Code current through Ordinance 2010, passed October 7, 2021 prohibits people from using vehicles, campers, or trailers for sleeping, eating, or resting on public property in the city between 10:00 PM and 6:00 AM unless explicitly allowed by posted signs. Additionally, camping, sleeping, or erecting tents or any temporary sleeping facilities on city-owned public property is banned entirely. Violations are punishable as infractions.
The city has a homeless commission, and its meeting on March 5, 2025 reviewed its Annual Work Plan for the fiscal year of 2024-2025, which was adopted April 3, 2024. The commission will focus on expanding housing and emergency support, strengthening community partnerships, and enhancing services for the unhoused. Key initiatives include: identifying new transitional housing sites, advocating for rental assistance, improving safe parking programs, collaborating with food and youth support organizations, and exploring mobile hygiene services. The commission will also seek funding opportunities, host community forums, and push for policy changes to prevent and address homelessness.
